entry.title: Why You Should Start Using the "Customer Return (A2X)" OData V4 API Today
entry.content:
Over the past years, we’ve invested heavily in shaping the next generation of customer return integrations through the Customer Return (A2X) OData V4 API. Compared to the Customer Return (A2X) OData V2 API, it offers greater flexibility, improved capabilities, and ongoing enhancements designed to support more returns scenarios.

Why Start with OData V4?

If you’re beginning a new integration project or exploring customer return APIs for the first time, we highly recommend starting directly with the OData V4 API. It offers:

Continuous Feature Enhancements

V4 is not just another API version—it’s where all new features and future innovations will appear. We are actively updating and expanding V4 to support evolving returns scenarios.

More Flexibility When Processing Customer Returns

V4 provides significantly more functional and technical flexibility than V2, such as:

  • Create returns orders without mandatory subentities, lowering payload complexity
  • Flexibly choose between synchronous or asynchronous modes:
    • Use synchronous mode when efficiency is a priority.
    • Use asynchronous mode when dealing high returns transaction volume.
  • Based on the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model
  • Flexible response handling
  • …..

Already Using OData V2? Why Consider Switching?

If you're currently using the OData V2 API, now is an excellent time to evaluate a move to V4—especially if you plan to support additional integration needs, such as handling configurable materials (AVC), automatic inspection, product valuation suspension, and so on.

While migration may require some upfront adaptation effort, switching from V2 to V4 typically does not mean rebuilding your entire integration. Most existing business logic can still be reused while adapting to the new API structure. Customers can also adopt a phased approach—continuing to run their existing scenarios on V2 while gradually introducing new or enhanced flows on V4. This ensures minimal disruption while unlocking long‑term benefits such as simplified payloads, greater processing flexibility, and access to all future innovations.

V2 vs V4 Feature Comparison

To help you make an informed decision, you can review the complete comparison between V2 and V4 in the help document:

Feature Comparison for Customer Return APIs | SAP Help Portal

Final Recommendation:

If you're:

  • Starting a new integration → Choose OData V4.
  • Expanding current integrations → Consider switching to OData V4.
  • Planning for long-term alignment with SAP strategy → V4 is the standard going forward.

The OData V4 API is already mature, feature-rich, and continuously improving. Now is the time to take advantage of it.

Resources

Help documentAPI hub link
Customer Return (A2X, OData V4)CUSTOMERRETURN

Customer Return (A2X, OData V2)

API_CUSTOMER_RETURN_SRV

entry.title: Re: Consolidation field from Group Reporting is unavailab...
entry.content:
Good day @junnipa_mapamoa411a5 

Thank you for your question.

The F2217 Display Line Items in General Ledger is not the standard app for showing Group Reporting line item dimensions like consolidation units, FS item, subitem category or subitem you mentioned. For those fields, the Public Cloud reporting path supports using the dedicated Group Reporting apps instead.

You should use:

  • Display Group Journal Entries for line item level Group Reporting review.

  • Group Data Analysis-With Reporting Rules for flexible reporting across consolidation dimensions.

 

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/90c07e91c7a64f328be3fd6b48955b13/36eeced62c0241188c63bf760e00240f.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/90c07e91c7a64f328be3fd6b48955b13/a0d3867e9351483e9349a4547331dc7c.html?locale=en-US

 

If neither of these apps fully matches your required layout, build a custom query on the released Group Reporting CDS views.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Inquiry: Does SAP Build Work Zone Support CALM-Based...
entry.content:
Good morning, 

All good except this "SAP Cloud ALM provides its own transport management support (for S/4HANA and other systems), but the documentation does not expli".

SAP Cloud ALM does NOT provide its own transport management "tool",  SAP CLoud ALM DEployment scenario integrates with these transport tools: CTS, CTMS and ATO.

We have currently different levels of integration, see in Integration of Deployment Tools | SAP Help Portal .

Then, from Cloud ALM the trigger of the transport actions to those transport tools is done. But the transport tool are in charge of executing the actions and send the feedback of Cloud ALM.

Best regards,

Dolores

entry.title: YCOA G/L Account Automatic Posting Assignment Documentation for COGS and Inventory Accounts
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,
I'm working on an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition implementation and need to understand the automatic posting logic for G/L accounts in the standard chart of accounts YCOA, particularly for COGS (Cost of Goods Sold) and inventory-related accounts.
What I'm looking for:

 

Comprehensive documentation or mapping that shows which automatic posting transactions (e.g., GBB - Goods Issue, WRX - Goods Receipt, etc.) are linked to each YCOA G/L account
Specifically for accounts in the 50x/51x/52x/54x/55x/59x ranges (COGS, consumption, variances, inventory changes)

 

Example accounts I need clarification on:

 

50100000 - COGS Raw Mat w/o CE
50150000 - COGS Packmat w/o CE
50200000 - COGS Trade
50300000 - COGS SF&F w/o CE
50301000 - COGS Direct Material
50302000 - COGS Third Party
50303000 - COGS Material Overhd
50304000 - COGS Personnel Time
50305000 - COGS Machine Time
50306000 - COGS Setup Time
50307000 - COGS Productn Overhd
50308000 - COGS Credit Co/By-Pr
50309000 - COGS - Hardware
50309500 - COGS - Licenses
51100000 - Consptn Raw Mat
51500000 - Consptn Packag Mat
51600000 - Consptn Trde Gds
51700000 - Consptn Repair Mat
51900000 - Consptn Subct.w/o CE
51950000 - Consptn Subcontr
52001000 through 52590000 - Various inventory variances, price differences, and adjustments
54011000 through 54444000 - Inventory changes and reserves
55100000, 55110000 - Production order adjustments
59801000, 59900000 - Adjustments and affiliated COGS

 

My specific questions:

 

Is there a consolidated reference document or SAP Help article that maps YCOA accounts to their corresponding automatic posting keys?
For COGS accounts (501xxxxx, 503xxxxx), which specific business transactions trigger postings? (e.g., goods issue for sales order, consumption posting, production order settlement, etc.)
For variance and price difference accounts (520xxxxx, 525xxxxx), what are the exact conditions that cause automatic postings?
Are there any Best Practice configuration guides that explain the intended use case for each account in YCOA?

 

I've reviewed the standard SAP Help Portal documentation for Financial Accounting but couldn't find a comprehensive mapping. Any guidance, links to official documentation, or practical insights from implementation experience would be greatly appreciated.
System details:

 

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
Chart of Accounts: YCOA
Focus area: Financial Accounting (FI), Material Ledger integration

 

Thank you in advance for your help!
Best regards,

entry.title: Re: FUE consumption calculation with development price ca...
entry.content:
Good day @s0016807163 

Thank you for your question.

What you are experiencing is most likely a refresh delay in SAP for me, not extra Developer Access users from other tenants. Note that Developer Extensibility and SAP_BR_DEVELOPER role are only available in the dev tenant, so this is tied to that tenant. The system collects licence usage through regular snapshots and it is updated daily, not instantly as you expect.

See the following on how licences are consumed.

https://support.sap.com/en/my-support/systems-installations/license-utilization-information.html

https://support.sap.com/content/s4m/help/reporting/licenses.html

Considering your question was posted on Wednesday, the daily refresh cycle should have solved that issue you were facing. If it has not changed after refresh, open a case with necessary details.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Setting up salary pay matrix properly
entry.content:
Hello,

I'm trying to setup salary pay matrix for compesation plan in Non-EC integrated environment. I have to include monthly and hourly rated pay grades into the matrix. 

I'm trying to setup the matrix in the following Scenarios:
Scenario 1. In pay matrix, I included the pay range amounts in their basic rate (ex. Monthly 1500 USD, hourly 14 USD per hour). Meanwhile, via the UDF file, I  also populate "Salary" amounts for imployees in their basic rates (Monthly 1500 USD, hourly 14 USD per hour) and also specify "COMPENSATION_SAL_RATE_TYPE" (monthly or hourly) and COMPENSATION_SAL_RATE_UNITS (12 in case of monthly and 2000 for hourly paid Employees)

Result: whenever I launched forms, the benchmark metrics (compa ratio, range penetration) worked improperly


Scenario 2. In pay matrix I converted the pay range amounts into annual values (simply multiplied the range amount COMPENSATION_SAL_RATE_UNITS). The UDF info remained Unchanged as per Scenario 1. 

Result: whenever I launched forms, the benchmark metrics (compa ratio, range penetration) started to work correctly

Scenario 3. In pay matrix, I included the pay range amounts in their basic rate (ex. Monthly 1500 USD, hourly 14 USD per hour). Meanwhile, via the UDF file, I  also populated "Salary" amounts for imployees in their basic rates (Monthly 1500 USD, hourly 14 USD per hour). But I did leave "COMPENSATION_SAL_RATE_TYPE" (monthly or hourly) and COMPENSATION_SAL_RATE_UNITS fields empty.
Result: whenever I launched forms, the benchmark metrics (compa ratio, range penetration) worked properly, but the system could not calculate the Annual salary amounts for employees.

Could anyone help me to setup a scenario In which:

1. I can include the pay range amounts in their basic rates (In pay matrix)

the system to calculate benchamrk metrics and annul ammounts correctly?

Hint. try not to use custom fields

entry.title: Re: DESDAV IDoc Fails with “Number Range Not Found” Durin...
entry.content:
The likely root cause is even though SNRO shows the number ranges as maintained, the inbound delivery type used by the DESDAV process is probably not assigned to a number range interval in customizing. This is why the system throws “Number range not found.”

Check the delivery type in the IDoc (segment E1EDL20 usually contains it), then verify in customizing that this delivery type has a valid number range assignment. That’s almost always where this error originates.

entry.title: Re: variant configuration, display values in cu50
entry.content:
Please try in the below sequence

  1. Go back to CU21 and confirm the constraint is released.
  2. In CU41, ensure the constraint is assigned to the profile.
  3. Verify that the constraint type is one that can derive values (e.g., table constraint).
  4. Test in CU50 again — the system should now propose values from the variant table.

In short, the green square shows the constraint validates combinations, but the missing proposals mean the dependency isn’t actively assigned or released. Once you link the table constraint properly in CU41 and release it, CU50 should display the values.

entry.title: Re: Create Product Variant from Sales Order - maintaining...
entry.content:
Good day @Oliver3 

Thank you for your question.

The two BadIs you identified are relevant for product variant creation from the sales order, so you can use them for product related enrichment during that step. However, I am not aware the Public Cloud offers a standard mechanism that allows you to assign super BOM or super routing during the variant creation event, neither is there a mechanism to automatically create or enrich the production version in the same step. See KBA 3354034 for more insights.

A supported design you can consider is to:

  • Model the configurable product with the super BOM and routing
  • Create the product variant from the sales order
  • Enrich your product variant data with the AVC sales order BadIs where the system allows.
  • Then create the production version afterward with the dedicated production version apps.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/a9f16584c6274ac6890aa2c3c5a7b6b2.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/907b678f57d24d3bbaa03f99e5506d21.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: QM-EWM Inspection Lot Summary
entry.content:
In standard SAP, you cannot cumulatively add stock from multiple inbound deliveries into a single inspection lot of type 08. Each inbound delivery will always generate its own inspection lot. To achieve cumulative inspection, you’d need either:

  • a different inspection type that supports summarization, or
  • a custom enhancement to merge lots or redirect postings.

entry.title: Re: MRP Type VM – Reorder Point calculated much higher th...
entry.content:
Yes in SAP Automatic Reorder Point Planning (MRP Type VM), the system calculates demand during lead time based on the forecast period indicator. If the period indicator is set to “Monthly,” SAP uses the monthly forecast consumption directly, without converting it into daily demand. This explains why your reorder point is inflated (~1.3M kg instead of ~487k kg).

How SAP calculates ROP in VM

1) Forecast Period Indicator (M = Monthly)
SAP interprets forecast demand as monthly buckets. When calculating reorder point, it multiplies the average monthly demand by the lead time (in months).

Example: 1,865,767 kg/month × (7 days ÷ 30 days ≈ 0.23 months) ≈ 429,000 kg.

However, if the system does not convert days into fractional months correctly, it may treat lead time as a full month, inflating the ROP.

2) Safety Stock
Added on top of the demand during lead time. In your case, ~52,000 kg is correctly added.

Observed Behavior
The system seems to be treating the lead time as one full forecast period (one month) instead of converting 7 days into ~0.23 months. That is why the ROP is ~1.3M kg (close to monthly demand) instead of ~487k kg.

Why this happens is because of period indicator mismatch. Lead time is defined in days, but forecast is in months. SAP does not always convert days into fractional months in automatic ROP planning.

Options to fix or work around is

1) Change Period Indicator to Daily (D)
Run forecast with daily periods so SAP calculates demand during lead time correctly. This will align lead time (days) with forecast periods.

2) Use Weekly Period Indicator (W)
If daily is too granular, weekly periods can reduce distortion while keeping forecast manageable.

3) Manual Adjustment
If business requires monthly forecast, you may need to manually adjust reorder point calculation or override system values.

entry.title: Re: When awarding the supplier quotation to PO, the inter...
entry.content:
Hi,

Currently, in the Manage supplier quotations app when users click the follow on document and create a Purchase order, the Manage Purchase order screen appears. This is a standard system design. So create the PO in the manage purchase orders app and then open the PO in the Create Purchase Order- advanced app to edit and add conditions on the header level.

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Dump Error - MIGO Background Job
entry.content:
Check SAP Support and filter according to your SAP version. For instance, searching M7437 MIGO returns all these SAP notes:

  • 1919899 - Error M7437 when posting goods receipt against inbound delivery in MIGO
  • 3056940 - dump MESSAGE_TYPE_X due to message M7437 during MIGO GR for inbound delivery (as already said)
  • 1661466 - MAA2: Error M7 437 for MIGO goods receipt for purchase order
  • 2379591 - MAA2: MIGO: M7 437 with a goods receipt with a multiple account assignment purchase order for non-valuated material in an HU storage location
  • 1756701 - MAA2: Error M7 437 for return delivery using MIGO

EDIT: by searching M7437 MIGO BUCHEN_AUSFUEHREN, I can also find these 2 other notes:

  • 3243140 - Local WM: Short dump with message M7 437 "Internal error: Routine BUCHEN_AUSFUEHREN internal table XMKPF is inconsistent" is raised - SAP for Me
  • 3169501 - Short dump with message M7 437 "Internal error: Routine BUCHEN_AUSFUEHREN internal table XMKPF is inconsistent" is raised - SAP for Me

entry.title: Re: BaseRefs and TargetEntries in lines instead of documents
entry.content:
Hi,

 

Every Delivery Note can have multiple base Sales Orders (and this is true for all the documents, not just Delivery Note). This is the reason why BaseType, BaseEntry and BaseLine are all stored on document line instead of document header. TrgetEntry and TargetType are maintained when a follow-up document is created (e.g. Invoice), so if a Delivery Note is your last document in line it will show only the default -1.

If you want to have a Delivery Note with more than one base document, you need to open the Delivery Note transaction, insert CardCode and then use "Copy From". There you have the option of selecting multiple documents and all open lines will populate the Delivery Note.

 

Br,

Matija

entry.title: Re: Customer Down payment work flow
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, there is currently standard workflow approval available only for supplier down payment requests, not for customer down payment requests. Customer down payment request approvals are not delivered as a ready-to-use workflow in the public cloud edition.

If your client urgently needs this, the fastest path is to set up a custom workflow in SAP Build Process Automation. This integrates seamlessly with S/4HANA Public Cloud and can replicate the supplier down payment approval logic for customer down payments.  You can also raise a feature request with SAP

entry.title: QM Inspection Lot with Segregated Responsibilities per Operation
entry.content:
Hi all,

I’m working on a QM setup in SAP S/4HANA and ran into a requirement I’m not sure how to handle in standard.

We’re using inspection lots with multiple operations in the Manage Inspection Lots app. The idea is pretty simple:

  • 1 inspection lot
  • 3 operations
  • Each operation has 1 qualitative characteristic (Yes/No)

What we’re trying to achieve is clear separation of responsibilities per operation.

So for example:

  • Operation 1 --> handled by Person A (Work Center A)
  • Operation 2 --> handled by Person B (Work Center B)
  • Operation 3 --> handled by Person C (Work Center C)

Each person should only be able to record results / confirm their own operation, not the others.

I’m trying to figure out:

  • Can this be controlled in standard (e.g. via work center, control key, or authorizations)?
  • Is there a common/best practice for this kind of segregation in QM?
  • Or would this require some kind of enhancement (BAdI, user-exit, Fiori adaptation)?

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Creating and Monitoring a New Replication Flow in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Introduction:

This guide walks through the end-to-end process of creating, configuring, deploying, and monitoring a Replication Flow in SAP Datasphere. Replication Flows leverage the ODP (Operational Data Provisioning) framework on the S/4 HANA side to extract CDS view data and land it into Datasphere local tables with full/delta support.

Steps:

1.  Navigate to SAP Datasphere – Data Builder

Open your SAP Datasphere tenant URL in a browser. From the left navigation panel, click Data Builder to access the central modelling workspace where tables, views, flows, and analytic models are created.

 

💡Tip:  Data Builder is the primary workspace in Datasphere. It houses all artefacts — Local Tables, Views (Graphical & SQL), Analytic Models, Transformation Flows, Replication Flows, and Entity-Relationship Models.

2. Create 'New Replication Flow' 

In the Data Builder welcome screen, locate the Flows section in the top tile bar and click the 'New Replication Flow' tile. This opens a blank Replication Flow canvas.

3. Source Connection Configuration

 

1. Click 'Select Source Connection' on the left panel of the canvas. This opens the connection browser showing all pre-configured connections in your Datasphere space.

2. After selecting the connection, click 'Select Container'. From the list of available containers, expand  and choose /CDS_EXTRACTION – CDS Views Enabled for Data Extraction.

 

 

 

Click 'Add Source Objects'. In the search dialog, type the CDS view technical name and press Enter. Check the checkbox next to the view name from the filtered results.

 

 

Click 'Select Target Connection' on the right side of the canvas. Choose SAP Datasphere as the target. This will persist the replicated data into local tables within your Datasphere space.

In the right-hand Object Properties panel, set Load Type to 'Initial and Delta'. This mode performs a full historical load first (Initial Load), then subscribes to ongoing changes (Delta) using the ODP subscription mechanism.

4. Deploy the Replication Flow

Click the Deploy button.  Datasphere compiles the flow definition and creates the target local table(s) in the space. Status changes from 'Not Deployed' to 'Deployed'.

After successful deployment, click the Run button (play icon) from the toolbar.  A confirmation message 'Run started' appears at the bottom of the screen

Click the Data Integration Monitor icon from the left navigation panel to monitor the Replication Flow execution status.

Conclusion: 

We have now created a replication flow to extract data from CDS view using delta.

Summary: End-to-End Steps

 

Action

Key Point

Open Data Builder → New Replication Flow

Entry point for all replication scenarios in Datasphere

Select Source Connection

ABAP connection type required for CDS extraction

Select CDS_EXTRACTION Container

ODP SAPI context; only extraction-enabled CDS views appear

Search, select, and add CDS view(s)

Supports multiple views per flow; partial name search works

Set Target: SAP Datasphere

Local tables created automatically on deploy

Set Load Type: Initial and Delta

Creates ODP subscription; captures history + ongoing changes

Name and Deploy the Flow

Technical name = system ID; deployment creates target schema

Run the Flow

Manual run or scheduled; 'Run started' confirms execution

Monitor in Data Integration Monitor

Track status, metrics, logs, and partitions per object

entry.title: S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (001) - SR(Service Request)作成時に確認しておきたいポイント
entry.content:
はじめに

S/4HANA Cloud Private Editionをご利用のお客様向けに、RISE側へ作業依頼を行う際に使用するService Request(以下、SR)のテンプレートの利用方法についてご紹介します。

SRの利用方法に先立ち、作業依頼前に確認された方が良い事項についてご案内いたします。
本ブログでは、SAP for Meを利用したSR作成方法を中心にご説明しております。

SRご利用前の確認事項

S/4HANA Cloud Private Editionをご利用のお客様が現時点で利用可能なSRテンプレートは100以上あり、多岐にわたります。
下記の内容を確認する事でRISE側に依頼可能な作業と各作業の内容がより明確になるかと考えます。
なお、ご不明な点などがございましたら、RISE担当者までにメールでご確認ください。

1. Roles and Responsibilities

S/4HANA Cloud Private Editionをご利用のお客様がRISE側へ依頼可能な作業内容は、「Roles and Responsibilities」にて定義されております。そのため、事前にご一読いただくことを推奨いたします。

また、「Roles and Responsibilities」は年2回改訂されており、3月にマイナー改訂、7月にメジャー改訂が行われます。

最新の「Roles and Responsibilities」は、以下のリンクよりご確認いただけます。
SAP S/4HANA Cloud private edition - Service Specifications

本資料をご確認いただくことで、RISE側に依頼可能な作業と、お客様側で実施すべき作業の区別が明確になります。

2. SRのService Description

すべてのSRには、Service Description(該当サービスにおける制約事項や実施内容等)が定義されています。

例えば、SR「Remote Client Copy」のService Descriptionには、クライアントサイズが300GB以下であること、ならびに当該作業実施中にSPAM/SAINTおよびCLOUDLMアドオンのアップデートが実行されることが記載されています。






































Service Descriptionをご確認いただくことで、該当SRの実施内容をより正確に把握でき、誤ったSRの作成によるプロジェクトスケジュールへの影響を未然に防ぐことが可能になるかと考えます。

3. SRの各項目のヘルプ

CSRの各項目には複数のオプションが用意されており、オプション値のみでは内容が明確に見えない場合もあるかと考えます。
その際は、「?(ヘルプ)」をクリックし、各オプションの詳細説明をご確認ください。












































4. SR実施におけるのダウンタイム有無

SR作成画面にて"Download data"をクリックする事でSR Templateがリストされたファイルをダウンロードできます。




 

ダウンロードした"Services.xlsx"ファイルの"Estimated Standard Execution Time"項目をご確認願います。
Downtime: サービス停止ありの作業
Uptime: サービス停止なしの作業

下記は、ダウンロードしたSRテンプレートのサンプルとなります。


 

 





依頼したSR作業期間中に業務への影響有無(サービス停止有無)は、重要なポイントとなりますので、作業依頼の計画段階にてご確認する事をおすすめします。

注意事項

  1. SRテンプレートは、日々見直しと改善が行われておりますので、SR作成時に都度ご確認する事をおすすめします。
  2. お客様側でSRを作成した後、RISE側で準備作業/作業実施/事後作業を実施するのが基本的な流れとなります。
    なお、SR対応中に担当者がお客様への確認が必要と判断した場合、SRのステータスが"カスタマアクション"となります。
    SRのステータスが"カスタマアクション"になった場合、お客様からの返信がない場合、後続の作業が実施されないため、SRステータス変更を確認し返信する必要がございます。
    従って、SAP for Me上でSRのアップデート通知を設定する事を推奨します。

まとめ

SRをご利用いただくにあたり、確認した方が良いと考えられるポイントをご紹介しました。
S/4HANA Cloud Private Editionをご利用のお客様がSRを作成される際の一助となれば幸いです。

次回からは、よく利用されるSRテンプレートについてご紹介いたします。

entry.title: Retrieve value from QR code via RF in EWM Public Cloud
entry.content:
Our pallet labels are placed from our vendors under a common format. There are several information that we would like to be able to retrieve in the RF transactions in our embedded warehouse management system. For the handling unit identification we are storing the box id on field External identification 2 of the handling units. We would like to be able to scan the QR code of the label and retrieve only the part that corresponds the the Box no and search for the handling unit number based on the external identification 2 value.

Is there an enhancement in RF transactions that provides this functionality?

--- Steps to Reproduce ---

Go to RF menu SAP . Select any RF transaction with HU (like create and confirm adhoc HU WT) and scan QR code with multiple information. 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Retrieve value from QR code via RF in EWM Public Cloud
entry.content:
Scanner configuration (with configuration software like 123scan or Data Wedge) could be used to parse the custom barcode on the device and pass only relevant information to our RF transaction. This would have to be addressed by the customer / partner based on documentation provided by their hardware vendor. For RF the result should be as if only the HU number (known to WM) was scanned. But see length restrictions as HU number is defined as CHAR20.

Theoretically, a logic as outlined in example 3 of this blog could be used to scan an alternative HU ID, let it be processed by a Screen Personas script, and then pass the internal HU number to RF, so that normal screen processing can continue. But see constraint mentioned in d) => string too long. Furthermore, the customer would have to do this on their own on (all) relevant screens and accept additional runtime for OData call.

entry.title: Re: Not able to connect with SAP HANA database customized...
entry.content:
Hi, 

-10709 message is for new connections.

If you check the timestamp of the 10709 error in SM21 / ST22 you may see a corresponding "rc=<error code>" exception in the Tenant indexserver trace file also.

These are covered in SAP Note
3059060 - How-To Troubleshoot SQL Error -10709 'Connection failed' in an SAP HANA Landscape

It is likely an idle or suspended cursor to the DB being terminated

Although an existing connection and 10807 is for already established connections to HANA which have since timed out.

"rc=10054" indicates a suspended or idle cursor usually :

3337961 - rc=10054 An existing connection was forcibly closed by the remote host
3220938 - Killed long-lived suspended cursor. tid=<>, connId=<>, duration(s)=<>, lifetime(s)=<>
3117294 - How-To Troubleshoot SQL Error -10807 'Connection down' in an SAP HANA Landscape

Kind Regards
Simon

entry.title: Re: Large Technical Tables /AIF/PERS_XML
entry.content:
Hi,

HANA Database Support maintains the tools and reports for as many of these tables as possible in KBA 2388483 - How-To: Data Management for Technical Tables

With SAP standard technical table cleanup, this is still is under the expertise of the SAP product owner and support team.
In this case for "/AIF/PERS_XML" that would be these teams for "Application Interface Framework"

  • CA-GTF-AIF (Application Interface Framework)
  • BC-SRV-AIF (Application Interface Framework)

So you should open a support case with them if you need assistance on the application process for data archiving of this table.

Kind Regards
Simon

Scenario

Thanks in advance for your help! 

Stefania

entry.content:
Hi all,

I’m working on SAP Commerce Cloud version 2211.43 and I need to configure outbound email sending via Microsoft 365 (Exchange Online).

Due to Microsoft security policies, basic authentication (username/password) for SMTP is being deprecated, so we are evaluating modern authentication approaches.

Our infrastructure team proposed using an Azure AD App Registration with:

  • Tenant ID

  • Client ID

  • Client Secret

(i.e. OAuth2 client credentials flow, without mailbox login)

However, based on SAP Commerce documentation (e.g. “Modern Authentication for SMTP Server”), it is not entirely clear whether this scenario is supported out-of-the-box.

 

Can anyone confirm if this approach is supported out-of-the-box in SAP Commerce Cloud 2211?

 

Thanks in advance!

Stefania

entry.title: Re: STO Billing (JSTO) Not Picking Price from Stock / Goo...
entry.content:
In Intra-company STO, there is no standard setting that makes the billing document automatically pick the material valuation price or PGI accounting value. SAP separates inventory valuation (MM) from billing pricing (SD). STO billing is treated like a sales process, so it requires SD pricing conditions.

Even in case of Intercompany STO, you have the option in copy control VTFL to pick ONLY the PO price. That setting is designed for intercompany STO billing (where the supplying plant issues an invoice to the receiving plant). In that case, the system can copy the PO net price into the billing document.

entry.title: Re: Onboarding New Hires Authentication using SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)
entry.content:
Hello! Does anyone know the answer to Heli’s question above? My company is in the same situation, and I’m trying to understand how IAS login emails can be re‑triggered.

We have cases where a new hire or rehire updates their email address, but we aren’t notified until after the welcome message has already been sent. In other instances, the new hire/rehire reports that they never received the email, and we need to resend it to a different account.

In the legacy process, we could use email services to resend the message to the correct email address. Based on my testing in the new process, it appears the only option is to cancel onboarding, update the email in Recruiting, and then re‑initiate onboarding.

I wanted to check with the group to see if there’s an easier or more efficient way to handle this as a SuccessFactors admin.

entry.title: Re: Poland 2 Mill PLN Threshold Withholding Tax
entry.content:
Standard SAP cannot automatically override DTT rates after the 2M PLN threshold.

You need either:

  • Dual WT codes with accumulation logic, or
  • Custom enhancement (BAdI/user exit) to enforce the statutory 20% after threshold.

My advice is to go with dual WT codes + accumulation rule if you want a configuration‑only solution. If your client insists on a single WT code that “switches” behavior, you’ll need a BAdI enhancement.

entry.title: Clearing, delete or clean up stock from storage type 921 TRANSFER and storage type 901
entry.content:
Hi all,

Background and Initial Situation

While reviewing open warehouse movements, I discovered a negative quant sitting in Storage Type 921 / UML-ZONE triggered by Movement Type 311. What makes this situation unusual is that LX23 shows absolutely no differences between WM and MM stock – so on the surface everything looks balanced, yet the negative quant persists.

The Negative Quant in 921

The quant in 921/UML-ZONE was originally created several weeks ago and currently shows a negative quantity. Its last movement was very recent, triggered by a fully confirmed Transfer Order. What is particularly confusing is the following: the Transfer Order that last touched this quant has two positions for the same material, both confirmed, both moving the same quantity from FRL into 921/UML-ZONE, and both writing to the exact same destination quant. This means a total of twice the expected quantity was posted against this quant. However, the quant still shows a negative value instead of becoming neutral or positive. I cannot explain this discrepancy.

The Transfer Requirement that triggered this Transfer Order has header status Processing Complete and is fully closed from a WM perspective.

The Positive Quant in 901

Separately, I found a positive quant of the same quantity sitting in Storage Type 901 on a fixed storage bin. This quant was created almost six months ago and has never moved since shortly after its creation. Most importantly, its TR Number is zero – no Transfer Requirement and no Transfer Order linked to it. It has simply been sitting there.

Are the Two Quants Related?

My analysis strongly suggests they are not related. The 901 quant originated from a production goods receipt several months ago, while the 921 quant originated from a 311 posting weeks later. They have completely different origins, different dates, and different reference documents. I therefore believe they need to be resolved independently and that simply posting a movement from 901 to 921 would be wrong and would risk creating new FI inconsistencies.

What I Have Already Checked

✅ LX23  → No WM/MM differences✅ LB10  → No open Transfer Requirements for this material✅ LS23  → Only ONE quant exists in 921 – no second quant✅ Transfer Order → fully confirmed (both positions)✅ Transfer Requirement → Header Status E (Processing Complete)

My Open Questions

First: How is it possible that a confirmed Transfer Order with two positions posting the same quantity twice against the same destination quant still leaves that quant negative? Is this a known SAP behavior when two Transfer Order positions write to the same destination quant simultaneously?

Second: When a negative quant exists in 921/UML-ZONE with a GR Date significantly older than the last Transfer Order that touched it, how can I trace the original event that created this negative quant? Which transaction or report gives the full movement history of a quant going back to its creation?

Third: What is the cleanest way to handle a positive quant sitting in 901 for almost six months with TR Number zero – no TR and no TA linked to it – without creating incorrect FI postings or new inconsistencies in MM or WM?





Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Credit check should not happen again, once Sale Order...
entry.content:
Hi User,

You can try using OVA8 Transaction Code where, for the combination of Credit Control Area, Risk Class and Document Credit Group, heading is available " Released Documents are still Unchecked". Here you can maintain the % deviation in the Sales Value as well as the No. of Days in which the sales order remains unchecked for certain days if those are released.

Hope this could solve your problem of sales order going for block again and again.

 

Regards

Ayush Kumar Jain

entry.title: Re: Enable Microsoft Copilot and Teams to Pass Requests to Joule

entry.title: Re: SAP FI – Document Currency Not Zero but Local Currenc...
entry.content:
Hi,

For foreign currency revaluation of non-open items, the valuation is performed at the balance level, not at the individual document level.

Therefore, when the foreign currency balance is zero, but a local currency balance still exists, the system will nevertheless select the account for revaluation. In such cases, even though the document currency amount is zero, the local currency amount remains and is subject to valuation.

Ideally, when the balance in the document (foreign) currency is zero, the corresponding balance in the company code currency should also be zero. However, if this is not the case, the system performs a valuation adjustment to align the local currency balance accordingly.

This behavior is explained in SAP Note 2406391 – FAGL_FCV Zero Balance Valuation.

As per the SAP note:

For G/L balance sheet accounts, if the foreign currency balance is zero but the local currency balance still has a value (for example, FC = 0 and LC = 180), the system translates the zero foreign currency balance to zero in local currency. To achieve this result, the valuation posting adjusts the local currency amount (e.g., posting –180) so that the final balance in local currency also becomes zero.

I hope this clarifies the issue.

Regards,
Ayush Kumar Jain

entry.title: Clean Core Compliance – Handling Dynamic WHERE and Dynamic Table Names in ABAP
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

We are currently working on aligning our custom ABAP developments with Clean Core / ABAP Cloud guidelines, and we encountered ATC warnings related to the following patterns:

  1. Usage of dynamic WHERE conditions in Open SQL
  2. Usage of dynamic table/view names in Open SQL

As per Clean Core principles, these constructs are discouraged due to potential concerns around security, stability, and upgrade safety.

Our Current Approach

To address these warnings while preserving required flexibility, we implemented validation using standard class methods:

  • cl_abap_dyn_prg=>check_column_name( ) — for validating dynamic WHERE clause fields
  • cl_abap_dyn_prg=>check_table_or_view_name_str( ) — for validating dynamic table/view names

After introducing these validations, the ATC warnings are no longer triggered.

Questions

We would appreciate guidance from the community on the following:

  1. Compliance Clarification
    Is the use of CL_ABAP_DYN_PRG validation methods considered fully compliant with Clean Core principles, or does it merely act as a workaround to suppress ATC warnings?
  2. Recommended Approach for Dynamic Scenarios
    In cases where dynamic behavior is functionally required (e.g., configurable providers, metadata-driven frameworks, generic handlers), what is SAP’s recommended best practice under Clean Core guidelines?
  3. Alternative APIs / Frameworks
    Are there any released and upgrade-safe APIs or frameworks (especially in ABAP Cloud) that can replace such dynamic constructs?

Objective

Our goal is not just to suppress warnings but to ensure that our implementation aligns with SAP’s long-term vision for upgrade-safe, extensible, and compliant development.

Any insights, official documentation references, SAP Notes, or practical recommendations would be highly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support!
Best regards,
Sunil

entry.title: From Securing Systems to Securing Decisions: The New Reality of Agentic AI
entry.content:
(Jana Subramanian serves as the APJ Principal Cybersecurity and Compliance Advisor, SEA. In this role, Jana supports strategic customer engagements on topics such as AI security and governance, cloud security, data privacy, secure data flows, multi-cloud security integration architecture, contractual assurance, audit, and compliance.)

A security model built for systems is now being tested by autonomous decisions

For years, enterprise security has been built around a familiar model: secure the infrastructure, protect identities, control network access, harden applications, and monitor data movement. That model served the cloud era well because most enterprise applications behaved in predictable ways. Developers defined the logic, and software executed it.

Agentic AI changes that assumption.

An AI agent does not simply process a request. It interprets language, reasons at runtime, invokes tools, connects across systems, and increasingly takes action with a degree of autonomy. That is why this is not just another extension of cloud security. It is a fundamental shift in the security model.

Why traditional cloud security is no longer enough on its own

Traditional cloud security was designed for a world where humans were the primary decision-makers and software was the execution layer. In that world, the key questions were straightforward:

  • Who has access to what?
  • Which network paths are exposed?
  • Which APIs are vulnerable?
  • Where is sensitive data stored or transmitted?

These questions still matter. But with agentic AI, they are no longer sufficient.

We must now also ask:

  • What is the agent being told?
  • How does it interpret an instruction?
  • Which tools is it allowed to invoke?
  • Should it act autonomously at all?

The risk is no longer only about access to systems. It is also about influence over the agent’s decision-making.

The attack surface has changed in a fundamental way

A firewall can inspect traffic. IAM can enforce permissions. DLP can monitor sensitive data movement. These remain essential controls, but they were not designed for a world where a well-crafted sentence becomes the attack vector.

In agentic AI, language itself becomes part of the attack surface. Prompt injection, instruction manipulation, unsafe tool chaining, excessive autonomy, and context abuse reflect a very different threat model from classical cloud applications.

 

In traditional software, code was the mechanism. In agentic AI, language can also become the mechanism of attack.

That is the tectonic shift.

What enterprises now need to secure

Agentic AI security requires a broader control model around how the agent receives, interprets, and acts on information.

This includes:

  • content filtering to detect unsafe or manipulative instructions
  • data masking to reduce exposure of sensitive information in prompts and responses
  • prompt templating to keep interactions structured and bounded
  • orchestration controls with least-privilege access to tools
  • human approval for high-risk actions
  • runtime isolation to prevent cross-tenant leakage
  • auditability for forensic visibility and accountability

These are no longer optional enhancements. They are becoming core design requirements for secure enterprise AI.

Why this matters in the SAP context

This shift is especially relevant in SAP landscapes because enterprise AI does not operate in isolation. It increasingly sits close to business-critical processes, sensitive enterprise data, and transactional systems. This makes governance of model access, prompt construction, downstream actions, and identity context far more important than in conventional AI experimentation.

As SAP customers adopt SAP Business AI, Joule, SAP AI Core, and broader AI-driven orchestration across the Business Suite, the discussion must move beyond classical infrastructure protection. The question is no longer only whether the platform is secure. The question is whether the AI-driven decision and action layer is governed with the right guardrails in place.

In practical terms, that means security must cover not only infrastructure, identity, APIs, and data, but also:

  • what the agent is allowed to see
  • what instructions it is allowed to follow
  • which tools and business actions it is permitted to invoke
  • when human approval is required
  • how actions are logged, reviewed, and constrained

The role of layered guardrails

A credible agentic AI security model cannot rely on one control alone. It must be layered.

For example:

  • Input filtering can help detect prompt injection before it reaches the model.
  • Data masking can reduce exposure of sensitive information in prompts, retrieved context, and tool outputs.
  • Orchestration policy can restrict which tools an agent can call and under what conditions.
  • Runtime isolation can help preserve customer separation and prevent unintended data leakage.
  • Human checkpoints can act as control gates for high-risk or irreversible actions.
  • Output filtering and audit logging can reduce unsafe responses and improve forensic visibility.

That is the core principle: no single guardrail is enough on its own. A content filter cannot enforce permissions. A permission model cannot detect prompt injection. A log can record an event, but it cannot stop it. Agentic AI security becomes credible only when these layers work together.

Why blast radius becomes a board-level issue

The real issue is not simply that AI agents are smarter interfaces. The issue is that they can become operational actors across enterprise systems.

A traditional application compromise may affect one workload, one database, or one business process. A poorly governed or compromised AI agent can potentially act across every system, workflow, and dataset it has been allowed to orchestrate.

That changes the blast radius dramatically.

This is why the security conversation must move beyond infrastructure hardening. Agentic AI security is about constraining autonomy, validating intent, limiting blast radius, and ensuring that sensitive actions do not happen without the right controls. The more capable the agent becomes, the more important these boundaries become.

Traditional cloud security vs. agentic AI security

 

Aspect

Traditional Cloud Security

Agentic AI Security

Primary focus

Securing infrastructure, applications, identities, and data

Securing the agent, its decisions, actions, and connected environment

Application behaviour

Fixed and developer-defined

Dynamic and runtime-driven

Main risk

Misconfiguration, unauthorized access, software vulnerabilities

Prompt injection, unsafe autonomy, tool misuse, decision manipulation

Attack surface

Networks, APIs, endpoints, code

Networks, APIs, code, prompts, context, tools, and agent workflows

Access model

Who can access what

Who can access what, what the agent is told, and what it can do next

Core controls

IAM, firewall, WAF, DLP, monitoring, encryption

Traditional controls plus guardrails, orchestration policy, isolation, auditability, and human approval

Blast radius

Often limited to a system or application boundary

Can extend across all systems the agent can orchestrate

Governance need

Access and configuration governance

Access, behaviour, decision, action, and data-flow governance

From concept to control: how SAP helps reduce blast radius in agentic AI

The security challenge in agentic AI is not only that an agent can generate responses. It is that the agent can increasingly orchestrate actions across enterprise systems, business processes, and sensitive data flows. That is where blast radius expands.

This is where SAP’s architecture becomes important.

With SAP AI Core and the Generative AI Hub, SAP provides a governed way to access foundation models. SAP documents the Generative AI Hub as a capability of SAP AI Core and SAP AI Launchpad that enables access to and orchestration of generative AI models within a governed environment. SAP also documents a Harmonized API that lets teams use different foundation models without changing client code. This matters because enterprise AI access should not depend on fragmented, model-specific integration patterns spread across the landscape. A governed model-access layer improves consistency, control, and policy enforcement.

On top of that, the Orchestration capability adds higher-level control points around the interaction itself. SAP documents orchestration as supporting modules such as templating, large language models, data masking, and content filtering, and describes the flow as including template rendering, text generation, and optional input/output transformations such as data masking or filtering. In practical terms, that makes orchestration highly relevant for input filtering, output filtering, prompt templating, and data masking in agentic AI scenarios.

This matters because security in agentic AI is no longer only about who can call an API. It is also about what context is passed to the model, what sensitive data must be masked, what unsafe instructions must be filtered, how prompts are structured, and how much freedom the agent should have in composing actions. In SAP terms, the combination of governed model access plus orchestration controls helps move the design away from ad hoc prompt handling and toward bounded, inspectable interaction patterns.

At the application layer, CAP and SAP BTP connectivity patterns are equally relevant. SAP documents principal propagation as the forwarding of a cloud user’s identity to a remote system. That matters because in agentic AI scenarios, downstream actions should ideally execute in the right user context instead of collapsing into a broad technical user. Preserving identity context helps maintain authorization boundaries, traceability, and accountability when an agent invokes connected systems. In simple terms, principal propagation helps reduce the risk that an AI-driven workflow quietly expands privilege across the landscape.

The architectural point is simple: in agentic AI, security must span multiple layers at once.

  • Generative AI Hub provides governed access to models.
  • Harmonized API helps standardize model consumption across providers.
  • Orchestration adds prompt templating, input/output filtering, and data masking controls around the interaction flow.
  • CAP and principal propagation help preserve user context and authorization boundaries in downstream actions.

That is how the discussion moves from generic AI guardrails to real enterprise architecture.

Traditional cloud security still protects infrastructure, networks, APIs, and workloads. But in agentic AI, SAP customers also need controls around how prompts are formed, how model access is governed, how sensitive data is masked, how outputs are filtered, and whose authority an agent is using when it acts.

That is how blast radius is reduced in agentic AI: not by trusting the agent more, but by constraining it through architecture.

Conclusion

Traditional cloud security remains essential, but it does not fully address the security model of agentic AI.

As AI agents begin to interpret instructions, invoke tools, and act across enterprise systems, security must extend beyond infrastructure and access control into filtering, masking, prompt governance, orchestration policy, identity propagation, and action boundaries. In SAP landscapes, that is where capabilities such as SAP AI Core, the Generative AI Hub, Harmonized API, Orchestration, CAP, and principal propagation become strategically important. Together, they help customers move from simply connecting AI to enterprise systems toward governing it safely.

The future of enterprise AI will not be defined only by how powerful agents become, but by how well their autonomy is bounded.

entry.title: From ‘What Is’ to ‘What If’ : Building a Dual-Model Payroll Impact Simulator for SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
In the SAP ecosystem, we often talk about "Meeting the customer where they are." When I started developing the Payroll Impact Simulator as a personal pilot project, I wanted to solve a universal pain point: the "black box" of payroll. Employees constantly ask how life changes—like a new baby or a 401k adjustment—will impact their take-home pay.

To solve this, I’ve developed a solution using a SAP Fiori frontend that communicates with Backend ECP OData APIs to trigger real-time simulations.

One Solution, Two Deployment Flavors

A key part of this pilot was ensuring flexibility. Depending on an organization’s landscape, the simulator can be deployed in two ways:

  1. SAP BTP Edition: The Fiori frontend is hosted on SAP BTP, providing a clean, side-by-side extension that leverages BTP's robust integration capabilities.

  2. ECP-Embedded Edition: For customers without a BTP footprint, the app can be hosted directly within Employee Central Payroll (ECP). This version is called directly from SuccessFactors using EC-ECP Single Sign-On (SSO) for a frictionless user experience.


The User Journey: From "What Is" to "What If"

The app is designed as an Employee Self-Service (ESS) Add-on that empowers users to visualize their financial future without risk. Even Payroll Admins can simulate the Payrolls for the authorized pay group population. 

  • Scenario Selection: Employees choose from various "What-If" triggers, such as office location changes, salary adjustments, or tax withholding updates.

  • Real-Time Simulation: The app triggers the SAP ECP payroll driver for the user’s specific country to calculate the result.

  • Instant Comparison: Users receive a side-by-side visualized comparison of their current "as-is" payslip versus the "to-be" simulated scenario.

  • Localization: For Every Country the UI can be configured based upon which infotypes need to be allowed for simulation. 

 

Why This Matters for HR Teams

Beyond the "cool factor" of real-time modeling, this pilot addresses three critical business challenges:

  • Transparency & Trust: Employees gain a clear "payroll story" and explore changes with confidence.

  • Reduced Administrative Load: By providing a self-service tool, HR and Payroll teams see a significant drop in routine "what-if" inquiries.

  • Data Privacy: A core design principle is that the data is transient. No simulation data is stored, ensuring 100% compliance and security.

 YouTube Link - Demo Video

 

Conclusion

This pilot demonstrates how SAP Fiori and OData APIs can be used to extend the standard SuccessFactors experience. Whether hosted on BTP or directly in ECP, the goal remains the same: taking the guesswork out of payroll.

 

I’d love to hear from the community—how are you handling "what-if" payroll scenarios today? Would a dual-deployment model like this fit your current landscape?

entry.title: Re: BAPI_PO_CREATE1 PO not selectable in MIGO – missing C...
entry.content:
Why ME21N works but BAPI does not is because, ME21N triggers the full pricing procedure automatically, pulling in PB00/PBXX and other conditions. BAPI_PO_CREATE1 does not run pricing by itself. It only posts what you explicitly pass in the structures. If you don’t populate the condition tables correctly, the PO will be created without conditions. Even if you fill POCOND, the system ignores them unless the pricing flag is set and the structures are consistent with the item data.

Commonly missed requirements

1) Pricing Flag
In the BAPI import parameters, you must set PRICING = 'X'. Without this, SAP won’t run the pricing procedure, even if conditions are passed.

2) Condition Step/Counter
Fields like COND_ST_NO (step number) and COND_COUNT (counter) must be filled correctly. If they are missing, SAP discards the condition lines silently.

3) Change ID
Must be 'I' (Insert) for new conditions. If left blank, the system ignores the entry.

4) Consistency with Item Data
NET_PRICE, PRICE_UNIT, COND_VALUE, COND_UNIT, and CURRENCY must align with the PO item. If there’s a mismatch, the condition is dropped.

5) Header vs. Item Conditions
If you only populate header conditions but not item conditions, MIGO won’t see the item as valuated. At least one item‑level condition (PB00/PBXX) is required.

entry.title: Re: How to add a check for requisitioner for purchase req...
entry.content:
You are right that the WORKORDER_UPDATE BAdI does not give you the full detail of operations/components at the right point in time. For your requirement, validating requisitioner before PR creation from a PM order, you need an enhancement spot that has access to both the order data and the PR creation logic before commit.

Explore with exits like IWO10009 (general order save check) or IWO10018 (specific PR creation check). Both have access to operations/components and allow you to raise an error that blocks saving/PR creation but keeps the user in the order transaction.

entry.title: Clean Core Alternative for Function Module [TRINT_READ_COMM_KEYS]

Dear Experts,

 We are working on Clean Core compliance for our SAP S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise system. During ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) checks, the function module [TRINT_READ_COMM_KEYS ] was flagged as internal/unreleased.

Current Usage: This function module is currently being used in our custom code,

Request: We would like to understand:

  1. Whether there are any released APIs or function modules available as alternatives to [TRINT_READ_COMM_KEYS]
  2. If there is a recommended Clean Core-compliant approach to achieve the same functionality
  3. Whether there are plans to release this function module or provide an alternative in future releases

System Details:

  • Product: SAP S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise
  • Purpose: Clean Core transformation and ATC compliance

We appreciate your assistance in this matter.

Best regards,
Sunil Hosur

entry.title: Re: PO not display in ME28 when using recorded script
entry.content:
This is a classic case of PO release strategy behavior rather than a macro/VBA recording issue. The reason your POs appear in ME28 but your colleagues’ don’t is almost certainly due to release strategy conditions not being met in their created POs.

My advice is to take one PO created manually and one created via macro, run report ME2N or check table EKKO, and compare the key fields used in release strategy classification. You will immediately see which field is missing or different.
